item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first three images could not be sourced reliably and are assumed not compliant with CC BY-SA 3.0.

The last image originates from here, which does not state a Creative Commons license. As such, it is assumed not compliant.

All images have since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why the interviewers wouldn't even consider that the subject might be right. I'm mean, Foundation personnel will regularly encounter as many as six impossible things before breakfast, so why balk at this one particular impossible thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I realise that this will probably be seen by no one in a short period of time, since the article is like 5 years ago. But I do have this question in my mind, and its been there for a very long time. It is regarding temporal, so I figured it will be good to ask it here.

How is the future determined?

To me, there are two main ways we can look at it. The first, the future has been determined, has been casted. It means that no matter what we do, the general outline of future cannot be changed, which is kind of like the case of 1968. The subject that came back from third experiment claimed that its the 17th (or is it 19th?) experiment. It means that no matter what the Foundation does, at some point of time there will be a 17th experiment. How it will be conducted is kinda beyond me, but yeah.

The second, the future is undetermined until it has been reached. Kinda like how 2003 works. We have lots of determinative sets, each of them possible to happen. It probably takes an event as a turning point to steer the future in that general direction.

But which one is the more appropriate one?

item.title: Re: what the
item.content:encoded: Seriously, right? Even worse, this was one of 3 or 4 sites I saw doing this with 1968.

item.title: Re: what the
item.content:encoded: LMAO conspiracy theorists are using SCPs now? grasping for straws so hard.

item.title: what the
item.content:encoded: I was looking for art on this particular skip, as I quite enjoyed it, when I found this. https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=QnmDV8liGvs enjoy.

item.title: Re: Keter?
item.content:encoded: Ignore this. Realized I was operating on misremembered info.

item.title: Keter?
item.content:encoded:
The "cover story" section shows the object only altering the memories of those who touch it, which to me really doesn't amount to Keter, more like Safe.

Also for the real article, saying "Hey maybe we can use this as a shot in the dark during an apocalypse, or maybe not because we aren't totally sure what it's doing" doesn't really amount to Thaumiel. Based on what we know, it's a disk that could either be displacing someone in time or into alternate dimensions, and at one point spat out a monster, that's a Euclid or Keter if I've ever seen one. Anyway, Good article, but both the "cover" and real story seemed misclassed to me.

item.title: Jr. Staff Post.
item.content:encoded: I am noting that I have reverted a recent edit that changed the object class of this SCP to Thaumiel. iamjj12, please be aware that your edit was outside the bounds of allowable edits for normal users. Please refrain from making changes such as these in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the device activates itself 'automatically', spitting out people from other realities… The 'reality revision' hypothesis seems fishy. If anything, it seems to be a mechanism of transit between subtly different timelines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hehe. no it wasn't on purpose. I fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume that the "Zackenburg" misspelling is intentional, as a self-reference to the SCP's nature itself. But if this is true, shouldn't the name "Zackenberg" in the image also be altered?
Really well done and interesting, nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article gets way to technical for me to actually enjoy reading it. I mean, it helps the tone and really sells the authenticity, but makes it a chore for me to read. I'm sure mot everyone is bothered articles that get really technical, it's just my preference. That being said I do like the core ideas behind all the technical stuff, so it's just a no vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ya know…shouldn't the first class be Keter? If not then at least remove the "(classified)" from the original Thaumiel classification?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have such a hard scienceboner. This was so well written and I could really tell you did lots of research. It's interesting, it's well-written, it actually technically makes sense, it's awesome.
This is a really good article and I really enjoyed reading it, good work!

item.title: Re: +200!
item.content:encoded: Thanks =)
I'm happy for you to have reached +200, but happier to have seen this SCP. It's great by itself, but leaves a lot of room for thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh thanks, thats very gratifying :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is phenomenal. Those images lend such an air of reality to the whole thing, and that's just the first compliment in a huge list of compliments I could give this thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really appreciate the footnotes in this article!
Definitely saved me from tedious Google-ing.

The article ideas itself are quite interesting as well.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is excellent! The science is spot on and really jives with the tone of a good SCP. The images reinforce the quality of the writing.

The idea that the universe has been reset by the "non-human entity" sent chills down my spine.

Maybe its my own love of physics that makes this one stand out so much but either way:

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is, to date, at the top of my favorite SCPs, along with 093. The implications it can have are mind blowing. I was thinking, using it in case of an end-of-the-world event could, theoretically, save humanity. But then again, it could end it forever as well. Change one thing, and some OTHER cataclysmic event takes place and destroys everything anyway. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks very much, Tldk. I really sweated this one, rewriting it many times.
Well, as far as Thaumiel, I've heard it two ways. I think the definition is evolving.

On the one hand, its used as a sort of super-keter which I think is self-explanatory. Given the scope of this SCP, I think it's appropriate.

On the other hand, I've heard Thaumiel used as a sort of anti-keter, or used to counteract an xk scenario. This SCP has the potential to be used that way, in an albeit dangerous manner.

I'm sure if anyone reads this post, people will express differing views, but at least in my head-cannon, that's whats going on.

Thanks again =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, you've done excellent job on the science stuff in this article. I found it a bit hard to understand all the concepts but I was definitely impressed and that's all that matters. It was really well written and I can see how much work has been put into this. Pats on your back for a great SCP.

Although I'm a little confused about this "Thaumiel" classification. I've seen it elsewhere but there is no official description for it in the FAQs or whatnot, so I don't really know what it is. Can you shed some light on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dedekind-uu class event. I swear some of these event designations just sound like you're making them up on the fly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just as I finished reading this one and went to upvote, wikidot very briefly broke. this rather terrified me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: good work. Plus one

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one screws with my head so much. I don't know whether to hate it or love it because headaches. That said, this is wonderfully well done and a wonderful play with causality. Many congrats and a +1 from me.

E: Did I mention research? This is one of those articles that uses research amazingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. The fake article that I would've upvoted on its own, followed by one of the greatest mind-fucks on the entire site. Just frickin' brilliant Requitefahrenheit. Take all of my +1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: vk event yaaaay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you,sir =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey. Hey, you. Yes, you, the author.

+1

Particularly for the animated image, the article, the idea and (all) the hook(s). I'll be going now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hehe. having started to read sf in the 70s, neutroniunm is always my go-to densest material, even though I've heard of quark stars.
i have dutifully amended the footnote to reflect this

thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The more I think about this, the more disturbing its implications become. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
minor flaw in footnote 1:

there is a theory that there is a state of matter between Neutron star (neutronium) and black hole(wich is not a state of matter).

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quark_matter
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quark_star

(whilst it is not anywhere near confirmed, it is a theory.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't heard of The Lathe of Heaven, but I should probably look into it.
If only that non-human entity hadn't self-terminated, maybe we would have understood it better.
Hopefully the Foundation has realized just how big of a change this torus is capable of making… maybe it's a good thing they didn't tamper with the glyphs.

The conclusion that the original existence was actually the one to which the non-human intruder belonged is an excellent bit of fridge brilliance; I only noticed it on my third read (I've read this twice before). An easily overlooked bit of logic that has some… well, world-changing consequences. *runs from SCP-504*

Also glad I could make your day :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic! I am so gratified that you got what I privately had in mind.
Thinking more about the real inspiration for this, I think about Ursala K. LaGuin's The Lathe of Heaven.
George Orr (a fantastic last name for a character that embodies contingency) is the only reliable narrator. The only one who remembers

And you're right. The first and second interviews never happened, really, not until that last incursion by the non-human intruder, the only really indigenous being.

Thank you

—edit

The way you put this, YourBuddyBill,made me giddy with happiness:
"…He stepped out of that thing and he saw a world unrecognizable. He didn't have a damn clue what to do about it. He snapped. And now we are left with what's left of the mess his people made."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a minute. If this thing is retrocasual… then it means…
…that it's altered it's past experiment logs in ours. The most recent retrocasuality would be the original direction that the universe came from, and everything else has been retroactively altered to match the rest of existence, up to and including the already-induced retrocasualities.
…Meaning that molecular what-the-fuck thing that had the weapon with similar design? That's right, his people created it, and he was selected to put it to the test, and some unknown number of retrocasualities later, the thing changed something back SO FAR that it completely erased his entire technology, species, and culture from existence. He stepped out of that thing and he saw a world unrecognizable. He didn't have a damn clue what to do about it. He snapped. And now we are left with what's left of the mess his people made.

We are so fucked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this one. +1
I can't believe I put off reading this for so long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was good! +1 It confuzzled me at first but as I started to get it I liked it. And then you went and did this:

The subject's memories would then be accurate and would remain the only record of the timeline before its alteration

and I went "…oh sh*t." And, from my extremely limited understanding of quantum physics, it all seemed spot-on, which is impressive. I can't imagine the amount of research that had to go into this. Bravo!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Or you can just wait until after you've read the article to post a comment?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a lot of material to read, which means I'm going to have to read it in parts at a time. My vote is currently pending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jeez….you're making my junk feel all funny
Thank you =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no idea on how to compliment you on this.

It's simply too beautiful!

*Cries*

Have my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Donut of destruction…-that- makes me think of the Simpson's episode, the donut of Damnation =D
"Let this donut be forever on your head!" (Homer's head is now a donut)
Homer, stop picking at it.
But Marge, I'm so moist and tasty!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unfortunately, none at the moment. I'm currently exercising my software engineering skills until I find a position better suited to my physics degree. But in terms of study, I've focused a lot on cosmology and quantum physics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you so much. that very much appreciated. =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This. Is. Beautiful.

All told, the elegance, and full spectrum of information that is intelligently conveyed in this article makes me marvel. +1 since I can not give more, this is my top favorite on this site.

I have to admit that my brain kept yelling "It's the Doughnut of Destruction!", no mater how much I told it to quit it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thanks so much! what kind of physics do you work in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a physicist, I LOVE the science content here. It feels absolutely dead on. The internal coverup is also effective to me. It conveys that this thing is insanely dangerous, but could also hold a level of temptation for someone. Your wife left you? Rewrite history! Got a bunch of people killed because you botched containment? Rewrite history! Yes, it's ludicrously dangerous and unpredictable. But some people will find themselves in scenarios where they just do not care.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If the Foundation thinks it's an open question, you may wish to explicitly say so somewhere in the super-secret-encrypted section, instead of passing it off as "fact" that it's altering the universe. It was certainly an open question to me, the reader, but I thought that the Foundation had made up its mind that it was universe-changing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the Foundation thinks its an open question. The near-instantaneous propagation of the virtual particle-pair production is 'consistent' with a global causality violation (global in this sense meaning universe-wide).
As author….yes, this is definitely what is happening =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great SCP! I also think you nailed the Sciency Tone and you seem to know a lot about physics (Casimir Effect, Virtual Particles, etc.) I gave it +1. At some point it creeped me out, at most points I was just enjoying the read.

I do have the question: did the experiments that the Foundation conducted "prove" that the Torus is indeed altering our universe? Or is there still a question as to whether its merely bringing in someone from another parallel universe? In either case, you definitely nailed the "Other universes are using it to reverse apocalypse" quite well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this skip. The concept was not only awesome, but it was delivered in the best way possible. My new favorite!
+1ed so hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. Hope you can get it to work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that's the one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like i've found missing picture. This is it, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. It hit me hard enough for me to +1.
